summary: DHH argues that web designers should work directly with HTML and CSS rather than creating mockups in tools like Photoshop that get handed off to developers. Drawing from his experience in the late 90s dotcom era and at Basecamp, he contends that designers who work with real web materials produce better results because the feedback loop is shorter and more authentic. He pushes back against the modern JavaScript ecosystem's complexity, which he sees as scaring designers away from implementing their own designs. At Basecamp, designers write HTML, CSS, and often first-pass JavaScript and Rails code, enabled by their server-side rendering approach with Turbolinks and Stimulus.
key_points: Designers who work directly with HTML/CSS produce designs that feel 'of the web' rather than merely 'put on the web', because the real-world feedback loop is shorter and more honest
The JavaScript ecosystem's growing complexity is regressing the industry back toward the old designer-developer handoff model, scaring designers away from implementation
Basecamp's server-side rendering approach with Rails, Turbolinks, and Stimulus deliberately keeps the stack approachable enough for designers to work independently
The notion that 'full-stack' is a point of derision rather than self-sufficiency reflects a harmful trend toward narrow specialization
Basecamp's designers routinely ship their own JavaScript and Rails code, proving this isn't a unicorn scenario but a matter of expectations and tooling choices
money_quotes: Not only would the designs feel like they were of the web, not merely put on the web, but they'd always be better. Less about what it looked like, more about what it worked like.
The towering demands inherent in certain ways of working with JavaScript are rightfully scaring some designers off from implementing their ideas at all. That's a travesty.

The calamity of complexity that the current industry direction on JavaScript is unleashing upon designers is of human choice and design. It's possible to make different choices and arrive at different designs.
Not going back to the dark ages of designers incapable of making their designs work on their own, incapable of making direct changes, and shipping them too!
Also not interested in retreating into the idea that you need a whole team of narrow specialists to make anything work.
Designing for the modern web in a way that pleases users with great, fast designs needn't be this maze of impenetrable complexity. We're making it that! It's possible not to.
key_insight: The modern JavaScript ecosystem's complexity is an avoidable human choice that needlessly pushes designers away from working directly with the web's native materials, and simpler server-side approaches prove there's a better way.
